common_name: Meader American Persimmon (Self-Fertile Northern Persimmon, UNH Persimmon)

planting.note: Plant grafted bare-root or container trees in spring in full sun in any well-drained to average soil; Meader is the most widely grown named American persimmon cultivar specifically because it is reliably self-fertile — a critical advantage since the species is typically dioecious and requires separate male and female trees — making Meader the practical choice for small properties or gardeners who can only plant one tree; cold-hardy to -25°F or below, adapted to zones 4–9.

directions: Plant in well-drained to average soil in full sun; Meader persimmon is drought-tolerant once established and thrives in poor, clay, or sandy soil where most fruit trees would struggle; grafted trees first fruit in 3–5 years, producing 1–1.5 inch orange fruits that ripen in October–November and become honeyed and non-astringent after the first hard frosts. Meader American persimmon was selected by Elwyn Meader at the University of New Hampshire and released in the 1950s as one of the first named American persimmon cultivars evaluated through a university research program, becoming the defining benchmark for improved American persimmon culture in the northeastern and upper Midwestern United States. The cultivar's most important characteristic is its self-fertility: standard American persimmon is dioecious and typically requires separate male pollinators, but Meader reliably produces fruit without a pollenizer — either through true self-fertility or parthenocarpy — making it the practical choice for gardeners with room for only one tree. Fruit quality is very good: medium-sized (1–1.5 inch diameter), bright orange, intensely sweet and honey-rich when fully ripe after frost softening, with a complex apricot-plum flavor that surpasses most wild American persimmon in consistency and richness.
sources: University of New Hampshire Cooperative Extension
Virginia Cooperative Extension — American Persimmon Production

common_name: Early Golden American Persimmon (Pre-Frost Ripening Persimmon, Historic Illinois Persimmon)

planting.note: Plant grafted bare-root or container trees in spring in full sun in any well-drained soil; Early Golden is notable for ripening in September — before hard frosts — unlike most American persimmon cultivars that require frost softening to become non-astringent; this early ripening is especially valuable in zones 7–9 where autumn frosts arrive late or for gardens where early harvest is desired; plant with a male tree or self-fertile variety for best fruit set.

directions: Plant in full sun in any well-drained soil; Early Golden is adapted to a wide range of soil types including poor, clay, and acidic soils; grafted trees first fruit in 3–5 years; harvest when fruits soften and color fully to light orange-yellow and test a fruit for astringency before consuming — fully ripe Early Golden fruits are sweet, non-astringent, and rich with apricot flavor. Early Golden is one of the oldest named American persimmon cultivars, with documentation extending back to the 19th century from Illinois nursery records, making it among the earliest formal selections of Diospyros virginiana evaluated for garden culture rather than simple wild harvest. The variety's primary distinction is its unusually early ripening season: fruit matures and becomes non-astringent in September — sometimes as early as late August in warm years — typically 4–6 weeks ahead of most American persimmon selections, and often before hard frosts occur. This early ripening is particularly valuable in warm-climate gardens (zones 7–9) where autumn frosts arrive in November or later, and for zones 4–5 gardeners who want ripe persimmons before early fall frost events. The fruits are small to medium (1–1.25 inch diameter), light orange to yellow-orange at maturity, with sweet, mild, complex flavor when fully ripe.
sources: University of Missouri Extension — American Persimmon
Illinois Natural History Survey — Native Persimmon Selections

common_name: Szukis American Persimmon (Large-Fruited Illinois Persimmon, Big American Persimmon)

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring in full sun in any well-drained soil; Szukis is selected primarily for its large fruit size — producing some of the largest American persimmon fruits available in a named cultivar, with individual fruits reaching 1.5–2 inches in diameter compared to the 0.75–1 inch typical of wild seedlings — grafted onto Diospyros virginiana seedling rootstock for drought resistance; plant with a male tree or the self-fertile Meader cultivar for reliable cropping.

directions: Plant in full sun in any well-drained soil; Szukis is drought-tolerant and thrives in poor, clay, or rocky soil; harvest fruits from the ground after the first hard frosts when fully softened and orange; the larger fruit size of Szukis makes collection and processing considerably easier than harvesting wild-type persimmons. Szukis is an Illinois selection named after the Szukis family, who identified a wild tree with exceptionally large fruit and began propagating it — addressing the primary limitation on American persimmon's appeal as a garden fruit, since the species' naturally small fruit (¾–1 inch) limits its practical utility compared to Asian persimmon varieties. The large fruit size of Szukis (1.5–2 inch diameter at full maturity) makes it significantly more practical for fresh eating, preserves, and persimmon pudding — the traditional Midwestern and Appalachian dessert made from puréed pulp — since larger fruits yield more pulp per unit of harvest effort. The flavor of ripe Szukis is typical of excellent American persimmon: intensely sweet, honeyed, and richly complex with apricot and brown sugar overtones, and fully non-astringent after frost softening. Trees are vigorous and productive across the range of Midwestern soils and climates.
sources: University of Illinois Extension — Native Persimmon Cultivars
Virginia Cooperative Extension — American Persimmon Production

common_name: Pieper American Persimmon (Named Female Midwestern Persimmon Cultivar)

planting.note: Plant grafted container or bare-root trees in spring in full sun; Pieper is a named female cultivar requiring a nearby male American persimmon or the self-fertile Meader cultivar for reliable fruit production; adapted to a very wide range of soil types and tolerates the clay-heavy and poorly-structured soils of the Midwest and mid-Atlantic states where most tree fruits fail; cold-hardy to zone 4.

directions: Plant in full sun in deep, well-drained to average soil; Pieper is adaptable to clay, sandy, or poor soils and very drought-tolerant once established; harvest by collecting fallen fruits from the ground after frost softening — fruits that separate easily from the calyx are ready; ripe fruit is completely soft and uniformly orange. Pieper is a named American persimmon (Diospyros virginiana) selection that has been propagated through Midwestern and eastern nurseries as a reliable female cultivar with above-average fruit size and consistent productivity — characteristics that distinguish it from seedling trees of variable quality. American persimmon is a dioecious species, meaning most trees produce only pollen (male) or only fruit (female), so named female cultivars like Pieper must be planted alongside a male tree or a self-fertile cultivar (Meader) for reliable cropping. Pieper produces medium to large American persimmon fruits (1.25–1.75 inch) that ripen in October–November following hard frosts, developing the characteristic sweet, honey-apricot flavor of excellent American persimmon at peak ripeness. The trees are vigorous, drought-resistant once established, and productive across the range of Midwestern soils — making them a practical native fruit for home orchards where Asian persimmon would be marginally hardy.
sources: Virginia Cooperative Extension — American Persimmon Production

common_name: Yates American Persimmon (Heavy-Bearing Virginia Persimmon, Wildlife Persimmon)

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring in full sun in well-drained to average soil; Yates is a named female cultivar of Diospyros virginiana requiring a male pollinator or self-fertile Meader for reliable fruit production; particularly noted for heavy and consistent annual cropping once established, making it productive for wildlife food plots — deer, turkey, raccoons, opossums, and many songbirds are strongly attracted to the fallen fruits — as well as human harvest.

directions: Plant in full sun in any well-drained soil; Yates American persimmon is drought-tolerant and productive in a wide range of conditions including poor and clay soils; harvest fruits from the ground after frost softening in October–November; the heavy fruit production of Yates makes it particularly attractive for wildlife plantings, permaculture food forests, and home orchards where a sustained fall crop of native fruit is valued. Yates is a named American persimmon cultivar known primarily for its exceptional productivity — consistently producing heavy crops of medium-sized fruits that ripen and drop over an extended period in October and November, making it valuable for both human harvest and as a wildlife-attracting planting throughout the mid-Atlantic and southeastern states. The extended fruit-drop period provides a sustained food source for deer, raccoons, foxes, opossums, and many songbirds rather than a brief burst of fruit, making Yates a popular choice in wildlife habitat plantings and food forest designs where the ecological role of the persimmon is as important as human harvest. For human use, the fruits are medium-sized (1–1.5 inch), deep orange to reddish-orange at maturity, with the intensely sweet, slightly grainy, honey-apricot flesh characteristic of excellent American persimmon after frost softening — a quality that exceeds most commercial fruit in richness when fully ripe. The trees are extremely adaptable across the range of Virginia and mid-Atlantic climates from zone 4 through 9.
sources: Virginia Cooperative Extension — American Persimmon Production
